Several approaches using embeddings have shown promising results on tasks such as link prediction, entity recommendation, question an- swering, and triplet classification. However, only a few methods can compute low-dimensional embeddings of very large knowledge bases without needing arXiv:1803.07828v2  [cs.CL]  9 Nov 2018

db1e7bd1-ad11-4e99-b57b-17b9bc272993.text: Instead of using a predefined scoring function, we learn it relying on Long Short-Term Memories. We show that our embeddings achieve results comparable with the most scalable approaches on knowledge graph completion as well as on a new metric. Yet, KG2Vec can embed large graphs in lesser time by processing more than 250 million triples in less than 7 hours on common hardware.

a842c852-888c-493a-b9cf-c3e1712c7dc4.text:
1 Introduction

Recently, the number of public datasets in the Linked Data cloud has signifi- cantly grown to almost 10 thousands. At the time of writing, at least four of these datasets contain more than one billion triples each. 1 This huge amount of available data has become a fertile ground for Machine Learning and Data Min- ing algorithms.

d251e65e-6773-429b-8c29-0d84986e240a.label: text_chunk
d251e65e-6773-429b-8c29-0d84986e240a.text: Today, applications of machine-learning techniques comprise a broad variety of research areas related to Linked Data, such as Link Discovery, Named Entity Recognition, and Structured Question Answering. The field of Knowledge Graph Embedding (KGE) has emerged in the Machine Learning community during the last five years. The underlying concept of KGE is that in a knowledge base, each entity and relation can be regarded as a vector in a continuous space.

645c9a8d-cc2f-4270-8e11-326b429ce75a.label: text_chunk
645c9a8d-cc2f-4270-8e11-326b429ce75a.text: The generated vector representations can be used by al- gorithms employing machine learning, deep learning, or statistical relational learning to accomplish a given task. Several KGE approaches have already shown promising results on tasks such as link prediction, entity recommenda- tion, question answering, and triplet classification (Xiao et al, 2015; Lin et al, 2015a,b; Nickel et al, 2016). Moreover, Distributional Semantics techniques (e.g.

e6ebba78-6ab2-45fc-ac48-2e7193ed6ecc.label: text_chunk
e6ebba78-6ab2-45fc-ac48-2e7193ed6ecc.text: , Word2Vec or Doc2Vec ) are relatively new in the Semantic Web community. The RDF2Vec approaches (Ristoski and Paulheim, 2016; Cochez et al, 2017a) are examples of pioneering research and to date, they represent the only op- tion for learning embeddings on a large knowledge graph without the need for state-of-the-art hardware.

3950aa4c-63fa-4d35-8e7d-ff1417f51669.label: text_chunk
3950aa4c-63fa-4d35-8e7d-ff1417f51669.text:
To this end, we devise the KG2Vec approach, which comprises skip-gram techniques for creating embeddings on large knowledge graphs in a feasible time but still maintaining the quality of state-of-the-art embeddings. Our evaluation shows that KG2Vec achieves a vector quality com-
1 http://lodstats.aksw.org

861f1faf-6829-4754-a84b-dbc701418398.label: text_chunk
861f1faf-6829-4754-a84b-dbc701418398.text:
parable to the most scalable approaches and can process more than 250 million triples in less than 7 hours on a machine with suboptimal performances.

2 Related Work

An early effort to automatically generate features from structured knowledge was proposed in (Cheng et al, 2011).

f2d68635-2029-45fc-b1d8-766886051911.label: text_chunk
f2d68635-2029-45fc-b1d8-766886051911.text: RESCAL (Nickel et al, 2011) is a relational-learning algorithm based on Tensor Factorization using Alternat- ing Least-Squares which has showed to scale to large RDF datasets such as YAGO(Nickel et al, 2012) and reach good results in the tasks of link predic- tion, entity resolution, or collective classification (Nickel et al, 2014).

baa2a43d-25ab-4eaa-b478-f0b4d1971351.label: text_chunk
baa2a43d-25ab-4eaa-b478-f0b4d1971351.text: Manifold approaches which rely on translations have been implemented so far (Bordes et al, 2013; Wang et al, 2014b; Jia et al, 2015; Lin et al, 2015b; Wang et al, 2015; Xiao et al, 2015).

bd500baf-15ad-45a3-8d35-46f4f0d574c5.label: text_chunk
bd500baf-15ad-45a3-8d35-46f4f0d574c5.text:
TransE is the first method where relationships are in- terpreted as translations operating on the low-dimensional embeddings of the entities (Bordes et al, 2013).

 On the other hand, TransH models a relation as a hyperplane together with a translation operation on it (Wang et al, 2014b).

b16bfb40-db08-4cbb-8159-e4f369d2f221.label: text_chunk
b16bfb40-db08-4cbb-8159-e4f369d2f221.text: TransA explores embedding methods for entities and relations belonging to two different knowledge graphs finding the optimal loss function (Jia et al, 2015), whilst PTransE relies on paths to build the final vectors (Lin et al, 2015a).

2c81fde1-d864-4699-a965-3d140b643173.label: text_chunk
2c81fde1-d864-4699-a965-3d140b643173.text: The algorithms TransR and CTransR proposed in Lin et al (2015b) aim at building entity and relation embeddings in separate entity space and relation spaces, so as to learn embeddings through projected translations in the relation space; an extension of this algorithm makes use of rules to learn embeddings (Wang et al, 2015). An effort to jointly embed structured and unstructured data (such as text) was proposed in Wang et al (2014a).

d56b7dae-cb4f-46f6-954d-4a82765dcaa4.label: text_chunk
d56b7dae-cb4f-46f6-954d-4a82765dcaa4.text: The idea behind the DistMult approach is to consider entities as low-dimensional vectors learned from a neural network and relations as bilinear and/or linear mapping functions Yang et al (2014). TransG, a generative model address the issue of multiple relation semantics of a relation, has showed to go beyond state-of-the-art results (Xiao et al, 2015).

39729be6-661c-4454-9f1b-7bcb4335f367.label: text_chunk
39729be6-661c-4454-9f1b-7bcb4335f367.text:
ComplEx is based on latent factorization and, with the use of complex-valued embeddings, it facilitates composition and handles a large variety of binary relations Trouillon et al (2016). The fastText algorithm was meant for word embeddings, however Joulin et al (2017) showed that a simple bag-of-words can generate surprisingly good KGEs.4
Tommaso Soru et al.

97fa6c37-4cba-4b95-b790-001348417575.label: text_chunk
97fa6c37-4cba-4b95-b790-001348417575.text: The field of KGE has considerably grown during the last two years, earn- ing a spot also in the Semantic Web community. In 2016, Nickel et al (2016) proposed HolE, which relies on holographic models of associative memory by employing circular correlation to create compositional representations. HolE can capture rich interactions by using correlation as the compositional operator but it simultaneously remains efficient to compute, easy to train, and scalable to large datasets.

a8109cbd-0f1b-4d58-9ab0-ea2d34751f64.label: text_chunk
a8109cbd-0f1b-4d58-9ab0-ea2d34751f64.text: In the same year, Ristoski and Paulheim (2016) presented RDF2Vec which uses language modeling approaches for unsupervised feature extraction from sequences of words and adapts them to RDF graphs. After generating sequences by leveraging local information from graph substructures by random walks, RDF2Vec learns latent numerical representations of entities in RDF graphs.

5578220c-7aa7-4939-bd17-7e90866e28e4.label: text_chunk
5578220c-7aa7-4939-bd17-7e90866e28e4.text: The algorithm has been extended in order to reduce the compu- tational time and the biased regarded the random walking (Cochez et al, 2017a). More recently, Cochez et al (2017b) exploited the Global Vectors algorithm to compute embeddings from the co-occurrence matrix of entities and relations without generating the random walks. In following research, the authors refer to their algorithm as KGloVe .

3 KG2Vec

This study addresses the following research questions:

1. Can we generate embeddings at a high rate while preserving accuracy?

2. How can we test the distributional hypothesis of KGEs?

3.

4cb898db-cd40-4fe0-adb0-b6c95819d3e7.label: text_chunk
4cb898db-cd40-4fe0-adb0-b6c95819d3e7.text: Can we learn a scoring function for knowledge base completion which per- forms better than the standard one?Formally, let t = ( s , p , o ) be a triple containing a subject, a predicate, and an object in a knowledge base K .

For any triple, ( s , p , o ) ⊆ E × R × ( E ∩ L ) , where E is the set of all entities, R is the set of all relations, and L is the set of all literals (i.e., string or numerical values). A representation function F defined as
F : ( E ∩ R ∩ L ) → R d (1)

059f4635-770f-484e-9fed-152920939628.text:
assigns a vector of dimensionality d to an entity, a relation, or a literal. How- ever, some approaches consider only the vector representations of entities or

242a2670-350a-4ce3-b828-2e1974d524c3.label: text_chunk
242a2670-350a-4ce3-b828-2e1974d524c3.text: For instance, in approaches based on Tensor Factorization, given a relation, its subjects and objects are processed and transformed into sparse matrices; all the matrices are then combined into a tensor whose depth is the number of relations. For the final embedding, current approaches rely on dimensionality reduction to decrease the overall complex- ity (Nickel et al, 2014; Jia et al, 2015; Lin et al, 2015b).

a74189f1-8689-4448-9d0c-a0e921594719.label: text_chunk
a74189f1-8689-4448-9d0c-a0e921594719.text: The reduction is per- formed through an embedding map Φ : R d → R k , which is a homomorphism that maps the initial vector space into a smaller, reduced space. The positive value k < d is called the rank of the embedding. Note that each dimension of the reduced common space does not necessarily have an explicit connection with a particular relation.

bc639746-e15e-4279-8b6a-07a01f46c674.label: text_chunk
bc639746-e15e-4279-8b6a-07a01f46c674.text: Dimensionality reduction methods include Principal Component Analysis techniques (Nickel et al, 2014) and generative statisti- cal models such as Latent Dirichlet Allocation (Jurgens and Stevens, 2010; ˇ Reh˚uˇrek and Sojka, 2010).

92175eb8-f3a2-4e77-8a68-e5fc343f64b5.label: text_chunk
92175eb8-f3a2-4e77-8a68-e5fc343f64b5.text: Existing KGE approaches based on the skip-gram model such as RDF2Vec (Ris- toski and Paulheim, 2016) submit paths built using random walks to a Word2Vec algorithm. Instead, we preprocess the input knowledge base by converting each triple into a small sentence of three words. Our method is faster as it allows us to avoid the path generation step. The generated text corpus is thus processed by the skip-gram model as follows.

87863e28-67ee-484a-8b16-79a6e99d1816.text:
3.1 Adapting the skip-gram model

We adapt the skip-gram model (Mikolov et al, 2013a) to deal with our small sequences of length three. In this work, we only consider URIs and discard literals, therefore we compute a vector for each element u ∈ E ∩ R .

c6e5a01c-055a-4542-bb94-50ba3f8e802c.text:

3.2 Scoring functions

3.2.1 Scoring by analogy

Several methods have been proposed to evaluate word embeddings. The most common ones are based on analogies (Mikolov et al, 2013b; Levy and Goldberg, 2014), where word vectors are summed up together, e.g.

084f5d04-fb54-49a6-bf63-4f70dc3ba8a5.label: text_chunk
084f5d04-fb54-49a6-bf63-4f70dc3ba8a5.text:
:
v [ ” queen ” ] ≈ v [ ” king ” ] + v [ ” woman ” ] − v [ ” man ” ] (4)


An analogy where the approximation above is satisfied within a certain thresh- old can thus predict hidden relationships among words, which in our environ- ment means to predict new links among entities Ristoski and Paulheim (2016).

2814f8b4-aca1-43b1-9c4f-3ab1220576b0.label: text_chunk
2814f8b4-aca1-43b1-9c4f-3ab1220576b0.text: The analogy-based score function for a given triple ( ¯ s , ¯ p , ¯ o ) is defined as fol- lows.

ae58c46a-c4ed-437c-80cc-f61420d0f458.label: text_chunk
ae58c46a-c4ed-437c-80cc-f61420d0f458.text:
score ( ¯ s , ¯ p , ¯ o ) = 1 |{ ( s , ¯ p , o ) ∈ K }| ∑ ( s , ¯ p , o ) ∈ K ( 1 if k v ¯ s + v o − v s − v ¯ o k ≤ ε 0 otherwise (5)
where ε is an arbitrarily small positive value.

bb2dde78-0d7b-493e-9cdf-ea20dce99969.label: text_chunk
bb2dde78-0d7b-493e-9cdf-ea20dce99969.text: In words, given a predicate ¯ p , we select all triples where it occurs. For each triple, we compute the relation vector as the difference between the object and the subject vectors. We then count a match whenever the vector sum of subject ¯ s and relation is close to object ¯ o within a radius ε . The score is equal to the rate of matches over the number of selected triples.

d3f42c8e-a704-4480-9869-db80a1b38274.label: text_chunk
d3f42c8e-a704-4480-9869-db80a1b38274.text:
3.2.2 Scoring by neural networks

We evaluate the scoring function above against a neural network based on Long Short-Term Memories (LSTM). The neural network takes a sequence of embeddings as input, namely v s , v p , v o for a triple ( s , p , o ) ∈ K .

9b5320da-9a31-4be8-8c4c-55d01a6788da.text:
3.3 Metrics

As recently highlighted by several members of the ML and NLP communities, KGEs are rarely evaluated on downstream tasks different from link prediction (also known as knowledge base completion). Achieving high performances on link prediction does not necessarily mean that the generated embeddings are good, since the inference task is often carried out in combination with an exter- nal algorithm such as a neural network or a scoring function.

e86153e0-2ab2-44cb-a3fa-b842555e6ddd.label: text_chunk
e86153e0-2ab2-44cb-a3fa-b842555e6ddd.text: The complexity is thus approach-dependent and distributed between the latent structure in the vec- tor model and the parameters (if any) of the inference algorithm. For instance, a translational model such as TransE Bordes et al (2013) would likely feature very complex embeddings, since in most approaches the inference function is a simple addition.

981b61e9-5397-49fc-ad8b-a855a95388a0.label: text_chunk
981b61e9-5397-49fc-ad8b-a855a95388a0.text:
On the other hand, we may find less structure in a tensor factorization model such as RESCAL Nickel et al (2011), as the inference is performed by a feed-forward neural network which extrapolates the hidden semantics layer by layer.8
Tommaso Soru et al.

23f89399-a644-4966-8990-c963b0d74309.label: text_chunk
23f89399-a644-4966-8990-c963b0d74309.text:
3.3.1 Neighbour Similarity Test

In this paper, we introduce two metrics inspired by The Identity of Indis- cernibles (Black, 1952) to gain insights over the distributional quality of the learned embeddings.The more characteristics two entities share, the more similar they are and so should be their vector representations.Considering the set of characteristics C K ( s ) = { ( p 1 , o 1 ) , . . .

3d9d9914-7487-462f-9830-fb34f32d63d4.label: text_chunk
3d9d9914-7487-462f-9830-fb34f32d63d4.text: , ( p m , o m ) } of a subject s in a triple, we can define a metric that expresses the similarity among two entities e 1 , e 2 as the Jaccard index between their sets of characteristics C K ( e 1 ) and C K ( e 2 ) . Given a set of entities ˜ E and their N nearest neighbours in the vector space, the overall Neighbour Similarity Test (NST) metric is defined as:

d260894f-7041-4e1f-a30c-427d9a409a6a.label: text_chunk
d260894f-7041-4e1f-a30c-427d9a409a6a.text:
NST ( ˜ E , N , K ) = 1 N | ˜ E | ∑ e ∈ ˜ E N ∑ j = 1 | C K ( e ) ∩ C K ( n ( e ) j ) | | C K ( e ) ∪ C K ( n ( e ) j ) | (6)
where n ( e ) j is the j th nearest neighbour of e in the vector space.

d23888fe-5523-4c46-a1c5-a7d768c94a6a.label: text_chunk
d23888fe-5523-4c46-a1c5-a7d768c94a6a.text:
3.3.2 Type and Category Test

The second metric is the Type and Category Test (TCT), based on the assump- tion that two entities which share types and categories should be close in the vector space. This assumption is suggested by the human bias for which rdf:type and dct:subject would be predicates with a higher weight than the others. Although this does not happen, we compute it for a mere sake of comparison with the NST metric.

13dd09b2-de0c-49b6-942a-03c97ac4ff03.label: text_chunk
13dd09b2-de0c-49b6-942a-03c97ac4ff03.text:
The TCT formula is equal to Equation 6 ex- cept for sets C K ( e ) , which are replaced by sets of types and categories TC K ( e ) .

4.1 Runtime

In this study, we aim at generating embeddings at a high rate while preserving accuracy. In Table 1, we already showed that our simple pipeline can achieve a rate of almost 11 , 000 triples per second on a large dataset such as DBpedia 2016-04. In Table 2, we compare KG2Vec with three other scalable approaches for embedding knowledge bases.

ce3a691c-be0e-4a89-98cf-e650cda31af0.label: text_chunk
ce3a691c-be0e-4a89-98cf-e650cda31af0.text:
We selected the best settings of RDF2Vec and KGloVe according to their respective articles, since both algorithms had already been successfully evaluated on DBpedia (Ristoski and Paulheim, 2016;
3 http://github.com/AKSW/KG2Vec
10
Tommaso Soru et al.Cochez et al, 2017b). We also tried to compute fastText embeddings on our machine, however we had to halt the process after three days.

9a6f94e8-c0f4-4e09-96bc-9ef869458fc3.label: text_chunk

4.3 Distributional quality

Computing the NST and TCT distributional quality metrics on the entire DB- pedia dataset is time-demanding, since for each entity, the model and the graph need to be queried for the N nearest neighbours and their respective sets.

 , e i } .
